<title>Evaluation of Greenhouse Gas Emissions from Five Swine Production Systems Based on Life Cycle Assessment </title>
F. Pelletier1, S. Godbout1, S.P. Lemay1, R.D. von Bernuth², S. Pigeon and J.-Y. Drolet³
1 Research and Development Institute for the Agri-Environment (IRDA) 2700 rue Einstein, Québec (Québec), G1P 3W8, Canada; e-mail of corresponding author: frederic.pelletier@irda.qc.ca
2 Michigan State University, East Lansing, MI 48824, USA.
3 Groupe BPR 4655, boulevard Wilfrid-Hamel, Québec (Québec), G1P 2J7, Canada.
1: <header>Written for presentation at the 2007 ASABE Annual International Meeting Sponsored by ASABE Minneapolis Convention Center Minneapolis, Minnesota 17 - 20 June 2007 </header>

Abstract. The goal of this paper was to compare greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions produced by different manure management strategies and using different nutrient to establish the farm manure management plan for a typical pig farm. Five scenarios were tested and the method used to compare GHG emissions was based on life cycle assessment. In scenarios #1 to #4, pigs were raised in a conventional building under liquid manure management. In scenario #5, pigs were raised in a building equipped with a V-shaped scraper installed underneath the slats. In scenario #1, the nutrient balance was based on nitrogen while, in scenarios #2 to #5, it was based on phosphorus. Results were expressed in terms of total GHG emissions and by two functional units: per kg of pig produced and per ha of land used. Total GHG emissions, in t COe per year, were 300.4 t COe for scenario #1, 448.4 t CO2e for scenario #2, 177.9 t COe for scenario #3, 278.7 t COe for scenario #4 and 200.5 t COe for scenario #5. The impact of the functional unit chosen to express the emissions and to compare scenarios had an influence on how the results were interpreted. If the pig produced were considered more representative, scenario #2 did not represent and interesting option in terms of GHG reduction while scenario #5 was the most interesting. Considering the area of land used, scenario #5 produced more GHG than scenario #2.
Keywords. Greenhouse gas, Life cycle assessment, pig production, manure management

3: <header>Introduction </header>

Over the years, nitrogen concentration in manure has been used to define manure management plan and application rates. As a result, producers have been over-applying phosphorus. This phosphorus surplus remained in the soil and was lost by runoff to surface water causing eutrophication.

In 2002, the Province of Québec in Canada has introduced a new regulation in order to ensure the protection of the environment and to reduce pollution caused by agricultural activities (Québec, 2002). According to this new regulation, by 2010, the whole farm nutrient plan of all livestock and poultry producers should be based on phosphorus.
To respect this regulation, different options are available to producers. For example:

<list>Increase the land area; 
Reduce the quantity of phosphorus produced by decreasing the number of animals; 
Export the manure on another farm in another region; 
Use a manure separation system to isolate the nutrients in the solid fraction, apply the liquid fraction on the farm and export the solid fraction outside the farm.</list>
Of those four options, the exportation of the manure and the installation of a manure separation system seem to be the most interesting solutions available to the producers.

von Bernuth et al. (2005) determined the efficacy of a liquid/solid isolation swine manure handling system installed at Michigan State University. The system utilizes a V-shaped pit floor with an adapted scraper installed beneath the slatted floor of the swine pens. Feces and other solids stick on the pit floor slope and are scraped to a collection point. The liquid including urine runs to a center pipe and is pumped to a holding tank.

The efficacy of this system have been tested in laboratory (Godbout et al., 2006) and at a commercial scale (Guimont et al., 2007) in the Province of Québec. Results showed that an average of 80.5% of the dry matter, 76.6% of total phosphorus and 54.2% of total nitrogen was collected in the solid fraction of the manure (Guimont et al., 2007).

In order to avoid pollution transfer when a new technology is installed on a farm, producers and all other stakeholders have to think globally. The installation of a technology on a farm (e.g. separation system) can have a favorable impact on the environment at a specific point in the production process and at the same time can produce a negative impact somewhere else. In that case, life cycle assessment (LCA) is a useful tool for the evaluation of the environmental burden of a product at all stages in its life cycle i.e. from the extraction of resources, through the production, use and final disposal. Life cycle assessment principles and guidelines are laid out in ISO standards 14040 to 14043 (ISO 14040, 1997; ISO 14041, 1998; ISO 14042, 2000; ISO 14043, 2000).

Life cycle assessment has been already used by researchers to evaluate the environmental impact of pig production. Basset-Mens and van der Werf (2005) used the LCA to evaluate the environmental impacts of three contrasting pig production systems in France and to identify hot spots for each system. The compared scenarios were conventional good agricultural practice (GAP) according to French production rules, a French quality label scenario called red label (RL) and a French organic scenario called organic agriculture (OA).
The assessed environmental categories were: eutrophication, climate change, acidification, terrestrial toxicity, energy use, land use and pesticide use. Two functional units were used to

express impacts: 1 kg of pig produced and 1 ha of land surface used. Per kg of pig, greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions were 2.30 kg COe for GAP, 3.46 for RL and 3.97 for OA. Per ha, climate change was larger for RL (5,510 kg COe) than for OA (4,022 kg COe) and GAP (4,236 kg CO2e).

Eriksson et al. (2005) used the LCA to investigate the impact of feed choice in three pig production scenarios in Sweden. Three alternative scenarios of protein supply were designed, one based on imported soybean meal; one based on locally grown peas and rapeseed cake and one based on Swedish peas and rapeseed meal complemented by synthetic amino acids. The environmental impact of both feed production as such and the subsequent environmental impact of the feed in the pig production sub-system were analyzed. The functional unit was defined as 1 kg pig growth, being an average kg of growth taking place in the interval of 29 to 115 kg. Greenhouse gas emissions obtained varied between 1.3 and 1.5 kg COe/kg pig growth.
Sandars et al. (2003) reported comparative LCAs for several pig waste management options. For example, various slurry application techniques, including: splash plates, band spreaders and injection.

8: <header>Goal and Scope </header>

This paper presents a combination of the results obtained in three studies. The objectives of the first study was to complete a theoretical analysis of GHG emissions of seven different manure management scenarios simulated for a 200 SOWS farrow-to-finish operation (Pelletier et al., 2005). The objectives of the second and third studies were (1) to measure and compare gas and odor emissions released from a barn equipped with a V-shaped scraper and a barn using a standard pull-plug system and (2) to determine the efficacy of a V-shaped scraper system to isolate nutrients in the solid fraction (Godbout et al., 2006 and Guimont et al., 2007).

The goal of this paper was to compare GHG emissions produced by different manure management strategies and using different nutrient to establish the farm manure management plan for a typical pig farm. This paper represents also the first step in the development of a simplified LCA methodology to evaluate the environmental impact of agricultural activities.
For the purpose of this paper, the following five scenarios were tested:

<list>1. Conventional pig building, liquid manure management and land application with a nutrient balance based on nitrogen; 
2. Conventional pig building, liquid manure management and land application with a nutrient balance based on phosphorus; 
3. Same as scenario #2 with the exception that the number of pig produced was reduced to enable the manure to be applied on the same land base than in scenario #1; 
4. Same as scenario #2 with the exception that the manure was applied on the same land base than scenario #1 and the surplus was transported and applied on another farm. 
5. A V-shaped scraper was installed in the building under the slats to separate the manure into a solid and liquid fraction. Both fractions were stored on the farm. The liquid fraction was applied to the fields near the farm and the solid fraction was exported outside the farm.</list>

Scenario #1 reflects the way manure was managed by the producers before the application of the new regulation in 2002 and scenarios #2 to #5 represent options that are available to producers to respect the regulation in 2010.

0: <title>Materials and Methods </title>
1: <header>Scenario's Description </header>

This study compared five scenarios applied to a typical pig farm in the Province of Québec. In scenarios #1 to #4, the pigs were raised in a conventional building under liquid manure management. All the manure produced was stored outside the building in a concrete tank. In scenario #5, the pigs were raised in a building equipped with a V-shaped scraper installed underneath the slats to separate the manure into a solid and a liquid fraction. After separation, the liquid fraction was stored in a concrete tank while the solid fraction was stored on a concrete slab under a roof.

For all the scenarios, it was assumed that the pig performances were not influenced by the building characteristics. Pig performances were taken from the results obtained by Guimont et al. (2007). In all the scenarios, piglets were supplied to the producer at an average weight of 24.2 kg and exit the building after 105 days at an average weight of 107.7 kg. The average daily gain was 0.8 kg/day and the death rate was 4.6%.

Characteristics of the manure produced by the pigs in scenarios #1 to #4 were taken form the value presented in CRAAQ (2005). Those characteristics are referenced values used to make nutrient management plans in the Province of Québec. Solid and liquid fraction characteristics produced after separation in scenario #5, were derived using values presented in CRAAQ (2005) and by using the efficacy of isolation measured by Guimont et al. (2007). Manure characteristics for scenarios #1 to #4 and solid and liquid fraction characteristics for scenario #5 are presented in Table 1.

7: <header>Scenario's Description </header>

This study compared five scenarios applied to a typical pig farm in the Province of Québec. In scenarios #1 to #4, the pigs were raised in a conventional building under liquid manure management. All the manure produced was stored outside the building in a concrete tank. In scenario #5, the pigs were raised in a building equipped with a V-shaped scraper installed underneath the slats to separate the manure into a solid and a liquid fraction. After separation, the liquid fraction was stored in a concrete tank while the solid fraction was stored on a concrete slab under a roof.

For all the scenarios, it was assumed that the pig performances were not influenced by the building characteristics. Pig performances were taken from the results obtained by Guimont et al. (2007). In all the scenarios, piglets were supplied to the producer at an average weight of 24.2 kg and exit the building after 105 days at an average weight of 107.7 kg. 0: <title>Methods for the Environmental Inventory </title>
1: <header>LCA and GHG Evaluation Methodology </header>

Environmental impacts of the five scenarios were evaluated using LCA based on the methodology presented in the ISO 14040 standard (ISO 14040, 1997). Greenhouse gas emissions were presented according to the specifications included in the ISO 14064-1 standard (ISO 14064-1, 2006) and the specifications included in WBCSD and WRI (2004). All the calculations were made using an Excel spreadsheet.

The GHGs for which emissions have been estimated were carbon dioxide (CO2), methane (CH4) and nitrous oxide (N2O). Greenhouse gas were expressed in terms of mass of CO2 equivalent (COe) using the concept of global warming potential over a period of 100 years which means that 1 kg CO2 = 1 kg COe, 1 kg CH4 = 21 kg COe and 1 kg N2O = 310 kg COe (ISO 14064-1, 2006).
2: <header>System Boundaries </header>

Indications presented in WBCSD and WRI (2004) were used to set the scenarios" operational boundaries. An operational boundary defines the scope of direct and indirect emissions for operations that fall within the farm's established organizational boundary.

GHG emissions were separated in scope 1, 2 and 3. Scope 1 included direct GHG emissions from sources owned and controlled by the farm. Direct GHG emissions were the results of the following activities:

<list>Building emissions; 
Storage emissions; 
Soil emissions; 
Energy used emissions (diesel and propane).</list>
In all scenarios, direct and indirect emissions from the soil were included in scope 1 because those emissions were from sources own and controlled by the farm.
Scope 2 included electricity indirect GHG emissions, which consisted of emissions from the generation of purchased electricity that was consumed on the farm.
Scope 3 included the other indirect GHG emissions and consisted of emissions from the following activities:

<list>Production of feeds, fertilizers, pesticides, seeds and piglets; 
Transportation of piglets, pigs, feeds, fertilizers, pesticides, crops, seeds, propane and diesel; 
Pigs and crops processing.</list>

For the purpose of this paper, only scopes 1 and 2 GHG emissions were estimated. According to WBCSD and WRI (2004), scope 3 is optional. In scenarios #4 and #5, GHG emissions produced by transportation and land application of the manure and the solid fraction were calculated but not included in the total. Those emissions would have been included in scope 3. Furthermore, GHG emissions produced by the construction of infrastructures (e.g. building and manure tank) were not included.

4: <header>Functional Unit </header>

Like stated by Basset-Mens and van der Werf (2005), GHG emissions were expressed by two functional units corresponding to the two main functions of an agricultural production system: the kilograms (kg) of pig produced and the hectare (ha) of land used.
Total GHG emissions were also presented. This presentation could be useful to quantify a possible reduction if, in the future, a carbon credit market is initiated by the Governments.
5: <header>Building Emissions </header>

Greenhouse gas emissions from confinement buildings include CH4, produced by enteric fermentation by livestock (Canada, 2006). In the Canadian GHG inventory, CH4 emissions from enteric fermentation of pig are estimated using IPCC Tier 1 methodology. The emission factor used in this inventory is set at 1.5 kg CH4 pig year*1.

Emission factors used in this paper are presented in Table 5. Those factors were taken from the results obtained by Guimont et al. (2007). For a conventional building under liquid manure management, the emission factor was 1.5 kg CH4 pig -1 year and for a building equipped with a V-shaped scraper, the emission factor was 0.7 kg CH4 pig year*1. Emission value obtained from the conventional building was the same as the emission factor used in the Canadian GHG inventory.

6: <header>Storage Emissions </header>

Methane emissions produced during manure storage in scenarios #1 to #4 and the storage of the liquid fraction in scenario #5 were estimated using the methodology presented in the Canadian GHG inventory (Canada, 2006). This method is based on the IPCC Tier 2 methodology (IPCC, 2000) with emission factors adapted to the Canadian conditions.
As reported by IPCC (2006), N2O emissions from the storage of manure in scenarios #1 to #4 and the storage of the liquid fraction in scenario #5 were equal to zero.
Methane and nitrous oxide emissions produced during the storage of the solid fraction in scenario #5 were estimated using the IPCC Tier 2 methodology presented in IPCC (2006).
7: <header>Soil Emissions </header>

Nitrous oxide emissions from agricultural soils were calculated with the methods used in the last Canadian GHG inventory (Canada, 2006). Emissions of N2O from agricultural soils consist of direct and indirect emissions. Direct sources are emissions from nitrogen that has entered the

soil from synthetic fertilizers, animal manure applied as fertilizer and crop residue decomposition. Indirect sources are emitted off site through volatilization and leaching of synthetic fertilizer, manure and crop residue nitrogen. 8: <header>Energy Emissions </header>

Energy consumptions (diesel, propane and electricity) on the farm were evaluated using economic analysis produced to evaluate the production cost of agricultural activities in the Province of Québec (CRAAQ, 2006b and C; CRAAQ, 1993 and CRÉA, 1999).

Diesel consumption was evaluated for all steps of crop production: manure agitation and application, tillage, planting, fertilizer and pesticide application and harvesting. Propane consumption was evaluated for barn heating and crop drying. Electricity consumption was evaluated for lighting and ventilation of the barn and for ventilation of crops during the storage period.

Greenhouse gas emissions generated by these energy sources were estimated using an Excel spreadsheet published by Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada (AAFC, 2003). 10: A basic uncertainty analysis was conducted on the results obtained by varying, one at the time, three parameters identified in the manure and the building CH4 emission factor.

Greenhouse gas emissions calculation for storage and soils were related to the percentage of dry matter and the concentration of nitrogen and phosphorus in the manure. The percentage of dry matter influenced CH4 emissions during the storage since the factor used in the equations for the calculation was based on the concentration of volatile solids in the manure. Nitrogen concentration influenced N2O emissions during storage and after land application since the factor used in the equations for the calculation was based on the nitrogen concentration in the manure. Phosphorus concentration in the manure influenced the area of land needed to spread all the manure produced on the farm and at the same time, GHG emissions.

Interval values of manure nutrients used for the uncertainty analysis were taken from CRAAQ (2005). For the percentage of dry matter and concentrations of nitrogen and phosphorus, the intervals were +1.3%, +0.4 mg/kg and +0.6 mg/kg, respectively.
The interval used for the building CH4 emission factor was arbitrarily set at + 50% of the values presented in table 5.

0: <title>Results and Discussion </title>
1: <header>Scope 1 - Direct GHG Emissions </header>

Greenhouse gas emission results of the five scenarios are presented in Table 8. Total GHG emissions, in t COe per year, were 300.4 t COe for scenario #1, 448.4 t COe for scenario #2, 177.9 t COe for scenario #3, 278.7 t COe for scenario #4 and 200.5 t COe for scenario #5. Per kg of pig produced, total GHG emissions were 0.81 kg COe for scenario #1, 1.2 kg COe for scenarios #2 and #3, 0.77 kg COe for scenario #4 and 0.54 kg COe for scenario #5. Per hectare of land used, total GHG emissions were 5.3 t COe for scenario #1, 3.1 t COe for scenarios #2 and #3, 4.9 t COe for scenario #4 and 6.0 t COe for scenario #5.

Values obtained in this project were in the same order of magnitude but lower than those presented in the literature. Basset-Mens and van der Werf (2005) reported emission ranging from 2.30 to 3.97 kg COe/kg pig while Eriksson et al. (2005) reported emissions varying from 1.3 to 1.5 kg COe/kg pig growth. The differences were attributed to the fact that GHG emission results presented by Basset-Mens and van der Werf (2005) and Eriksson et al. (2005) were obtained from complete LCAs that included more emission sources like piglets and feed production.

In scenarios #4 and #5, GHG emissions associated with transportation and crop production after land application of the manure surplus or the solid fraction were not included in the total of the scenario. In a complete LCA, those emissions would have been included in scope 3. In scenario #4, these emissions accounted for 5.4 t COe for transportation of the manure over a distance of 30 km and 169.7 t COe for crop production. In scenario #5, these emissions accounted for 0.97 t COe for transportation of the solid fraction over a distance of 50 km and 85.3 t COe for crop production.

In all the scenarios, building emissions represented 9 to 15% of the total GHG emissions. Emissions associated with manure storage represented between 26 and 45% of the total. Direct and indirect emissions from the soils accounted for between 32 and 51% of the total. In proportion, emissions from the soils were more important in scenarios #2 and #3 where land use was higher. Finally, emissions associated with the energy consumption represented between 11 and 14% of the total.

In all the five scenarios, scope 2, electricity indirect GHG emissions, represented less than 0.1% of the total GHG emissions. Since most of the electricity produced in the Province of Québec comes from hydro-electricity the emission factor was very low: 0.067 g CH4/KWh or 0.0014 kg CO2e/kWh. Results would have been higher only by using the average emission factor for electricity produced in Canada, which is 0.22 kg CO2e/kWh (AAFC, 2003).
5: <header>Nitrogen Fertilizer Consumption </header>

Urea was used as a nitrogen fertilizer to complete the nutrient management plan in scenarios #2 to #5. On average, 131 kg urea/ha were used in scenarios #2, #3 and #4 while 26 kg urea/ha were used in scenario #5. 0: <title>Comparison of the Scenarios </title>
Scenario #2 vs Scenario #1
Scenarios #1 and #2 illustrates the comparison of nutrient balances based on phosphorus or nitrogen.

Total GHG emissions increased by 148 t COe from scenario #1 to scenario #2. Direct and indirect emissions from the soil increased by 94.5 t COe and 24.2 t COe, respectively, while emissions from the energy increased by 29.3 t COe between scenario #1 and scenario #2. This difference was caused by the increase of land used in scenario #2 from 56.6 to 142.4 ha and by the increase of nitrogen fertilizer used from 0 to 131 kg urea/ha.

Per kg of pig produced, emissions increased of 0.4 kg COe while per ha of land emissions decreased of 2.2 t COe. This difference was explained by the fact that for the same number of pig produced in both scenarios, the area of land used was more important in scenario #2.

In terms of pig produced, the nutrient balance of scenario #2, based on phosphorus, produced more GHG compare to the nutrient balance of scenario #2, based on nitrogen. However, in terms of ha of land used, the nutrient management plan of scenario #2 produced less GHG emissions.

2: <header>Scenario #3 vs Scenarios #2 and #1 </header>

In scenario #3, the producer would have to reduce his production by 60% to be able to spread the manure produced on a phosphorus balance and on the same area of land used in scenario #1. From an economic perspective, this scenario may not be a realistic and viable alternative for a producer.

Greenhouse gas emission reductions were observed in all the stages of the production. Per kg of pig produced and per ha of land used, emissions from scenarios #2 and #3 were identical because the proportion of pigs and the area of land remained the same. Furthermore, the quantity of nitrogen fertilizer used per ha in scenario #3 was identical to the quantity used in scenario #2.
3: <header>Scenario #4 vs Scenarios #2 and #1 </header>
Scenario #4 represents an interesting alternative for a producer with a manure surplus if there is another producer able to receive and apply the manure.

Considering producer A alone, GHG emissions per kg of pig and per ha of land were lower in scenario #4 compared to scenario #1. If emissions caused by transportation of the manure were included, total GHG emissions of producers A and B were higher in scenario #4 compared to scenario #2.
A more detailed LCA, including the activities of producer B, should be done in order to conclude if GHG emissions were increased in scenario #4.
4: <header>Scenario #5 vs Scenarios #1, #2 and #4 </header>

According to the results obtained, manure separation in the building seems to be an interesting alternative to reduce GHG emissions. Considering only producer A, a reduction of 100 t COe was observed between scenarios #5 and #1, 248 t COe between scenarios #5 and #2 and
78 t COe between scenarios #5 and #4. The GHG reduction was observed in all stages of the production: building, storage, soils and energy used.
Per kg of pig produced, emissions decreased by 0.27 kg COe while per ha of land emissions increased by 0.7 t COe.
Building emissions were cut by 53%. This reduction was directly associated with the CH4 emission factor used.

Storage of the liquid fraction after separation produced 22.5 t COe compare to 115.3 t COe for the storage of manure. This reduction of 80.5% was directly associated with the reduction of dry matter in the liquid fraction which was associated with the efficacy of the V-shaped scraper system to isolate nutrients in the solid fraction.

Emissions associated with the storage of the solid fraction were equal to 68.2 t COe. Reported in COe, 4% of the emissions were associated to CH4 and 96% to N2O. The efficacy of the V- shaped scraper system to isolate nitrogen in the solid fraction combine with the N2O emission factor used can have an important impact on the estimation of the emissions. Methane emissions are related to the percentage of dry matter while N2O emissions are related to the concentration of nitrogen in the solid fraction.

In scenario #5, the quantity of fertilizer applied on the fields was on average 26 kg urea/ha. A reduction of around 105 kg urea/ha compared to scenarios #2, #3 and #4. This reduction was due to a more balance N/P ratio in the liquid fraction after separation.

Like scenario #4, a more detailed LCA should be completed in order to conclude in a reduction of GHG emissions by scenario #5. The activities of producer B should also be included in the analysis. Furthermore, the management of the solid fraction is still under development. More research should be done to characterize this fraction and to developed different options for the producers.

7: <header>Influence of the Functional Unit </header>

The impact of the functional unit chosen to express the emissions and to compare the scenarios had an influence on how the results were interpreted. If the pig produced were considered more representative, scenario #2 did not represent and interesting option in terms of GHG reduction while scenario #5 was the most interesting. Considering the area of land used, scenario #5 produced more GHG than scenario #2.

The analysis should be based on what is more representative of the production. The pigs produced represent money for the producers and may be the most important variable nowadays. Considering the fact that the area of land use for agricultural activities tend to decrease the land may not be a renewable resource and land use should be considered as an important variable.
8: <header>Uncertainty </header>

Greenhouse gas emission variations results for scenarios #2 and #5 are presented in table 9. Variation coefficients have been applied one at the time to the building emission factor and to the percentage of dry matter, nitrogen concentration and phosphorus concentration in the manure.
On the total GHG emissions, a variation of + 50% applied on the building emission factor had an impact of + 4.5% on scenario #2 and + 4.7% on scenario #5.

For scenario #2, a variation of + 1.3% applied on the percentage of dry matter in the manure had a direct impact of + 26.5% on the manure storage emissions and an impact of + 6.8% on the total GHG emissions. For scenario #5, the same variation had a direct impact of + 26.5% on the liquid fraction storage and of + 1.0% on the solid fraction storage. In that case, the impact on the total GHG emissions was + 3.5%.

On the total GHG emissions, a variation of + 0.4 mg/kg applied on the concentration of nitrogen in the manure had an impact of + 0.55% on scenario #2 and + 3.5% on scenario #5. The nitrogen concentration in the manure influenced the quantity of nitrogen fertilizer required by the nutrient management plan. The impact on the total GHG emissions was relatively small since the quantity of nitrogen required by the cultures remained the same.
In scenario #5, emissions from the storage of the solid fraction increased or decreased by 8.6% with the variation of the nitrogen concentration in the manure.

Phosphorus concentration variation in the manure had the most important impact on GHG emissions: + 14.6% in scenario #2 and + 7.9% in scenario #5. Those variations were attributed to the variation of land needed to spread the manure since the nutrient balance in both scenarios was based on phosphorus. The variation of land had a direct impact on the soils emissions and on the energy consumption. The + 0.6 mg/kg phosphorus concentration variation in the manure produced a variation of + 26.1% in the area of land needed in both scenarios.

10: <header>Limitations and What Needs to be Done Next? </header>

The evaluation presented in this paper should be seen as an approximation made with a theoretical case with theoretical values. It is not possible to apply the results obtained to an entire production sector and since the estimation was made using values, methods and emission factors that came from different sources it may not represent the exact GHG emissions. Furthermore, validation and verification processes imposed by the ISO 14064-1 have not been made.

The decision of whether or not a technology or a new management method should be applied on a farm should not only be based on GHG emission results. In order to compare the environmental impact of the scenarios, a complete LCA including impact categories like acidification, eutrophication and terrestrial ecotoxicity should be made. The introduction of a separation system, like in scenario #5, can have an influence on those categories. For example, losses of NH3, NO, PO4 and SO2 in air, soil and water can create eutrophication and acidification. Concentrations of metals like cadmium, copper, nickel, lead and zinc in manure or solid and liquid fractions after separation can also have an important impact on the terrestrial ecotoxicity. A separation system will concentrate those elements in the solid fraction and the application of this fraction on a reduced area of land will increased their concentrations in the soil. More research should be conducted to better understand the losses caused by the management of both liquid and solid fractions.
An economic evaluation should also be made in order to associate a cost to each scenario.

13: <header>Limitations and What Needs to be Done Next? </header>

The evaluation presented in this paper should be seen as an approximation made with a theoretical case with theoretical values. It is not possible to apply the results obtained to an entire production sector and since the estimation was made using values, methods and emission factors that came from different sources it may not represent the exact GHG emissions. Furthermore, validation and verification processes imposed by the ISO 14064-1 have not been made.

The decision of whether or not a technology or a new management method should be applied on a farm should not only be based on GHG emission results. In order to compare the environmental impact of the scenarios, a complete LCA including impact categories like acidification, eutrophication and terrestrial ecotoxicity should be made. The introduction of a separation system, like in scenario #5, can have an influence on those categories. For example, losses of NH3, NO, PO4 and SO2 in air, soil and water can create eutrophication and acidification. Concentrations of metals like cadmium, copper, nickel, lead and zinc in manure or solid and liquid fractions after separation can also have an important impact on the terrestrial ecotoxicity. A separation system will concentrate those elements in the solid fraction and the application of this fraction on a reduced area of land will increased their concentrations in the soil. More research should be conducted to better understand the losses caused by the management of both liquid and solid fractions.
An economic evaluation should also be made in order to associate a cost to each scenario.

14: The goal of this paper was to compare GHG emissions produced by different manure management strategies and different nutrient balances applied to a typical pig farm. Five scenarios were tested and the method used to compare the GHG emissions was based on LCA.

In scenarios #1 to #4, pigs were raised in a conventional building under liquid manure management. In scenario #5, pigs were raised in a building equipped with a V-shaped scraper installed underneath the slats. In scenario #1, the nutrient balance was based on nitrogen while, in scenarios #2 to #5, it was based on phosphorus.

Results were expressed in terms of total GHG emissions and with two functional units: per kg of pig produced and per ha of land used. Total GHG emissions, in t COe per year, were 300.4 t COe for scenario #1, 448.4 t COe for scenario #2, 177.9 t COe for scenario #3, 278.7 t COe for scenario #4 and 200.5 t COe for scenario #5. Per kg of pig produced, total GHG emissions were 0.81 kg CO2e for scenario #1, 1.2 kg COe for scenarios #2 and #3, 0.77 kg COe for scenario #4 and 0.54 kg COe for scenario #5. Per hectare of land used, total GHG emissions were 5.3 t COe for scenario #1, 3.1 t COe for scenarios #2 and #3, 4.9 t COe for scenario #4 and 6.0 COe for scenario #5.

The impact of the functional unit chosen to express the emissions and to compare the scenarios had an influence on how the results were interpreted. If the pig produced were considered more representative, scenario #2 did not represent and interesting option in terms of GHG reduction while scenario #5 was the most interesting. Considering the area of land used, scenario #5 produced more GHG than scenario #2.

The decision of whether or not a technology or a new management method should be applied on a farm should not only be based on GHG emission results. In order to compare the environmental impact of the scenarios, a complete LCA including impact categories like acidification, eutrophication and terrestrial ecotoxicity should be made.
